pi_items.source: CDC Oswego outbreak investigation, 1951 EIS training case - 75 of 80 attendees interviewed at church supper on April 18, 1940
epitools R package oswego documentation shows sex variable with F (Female) and M (Male) coding in raw data; task standardizes to full text 'Male'/'Female'
epitools R package oswego documentation shows ill variable with Y (Yes) and N (No) coding in raw data; task standardizes to full text 'Yes'/'No'

No evidence found in public Oswego dataset documentation (epitools, epiDisplay, CDC resources). The 999 sentinel value appears to be specific to this task's artificially-created messy CSV version, not present in documented source data.
Windows environment-specific path; cannot verify without accessing the actual Windows Docker environment

privileged_info_summary: This task is based on the real 1940 Oswego gastroenteritis outbreak, a classic epidemiological case study from the CDC's Epidemic Intelligence Service training. The original dataset contains 75 observations of people interviewed from the church supper event. The metadata correctly specifies that 75 rows should be present in the input data. The sex variable standardization targets are verified as 'Male' and 'Female' (converted from abbreviated F/M in the original data), and the ill variable standardization targets are verified as 'Yes' and 'No' (converted from abbreviated Y/N). The sentinel value of 999 for age is not documented in public sources and appears to be task-specific. The data is synthetic in that oswego_messy.csv is an artificially created messy version of the real Oswego dataset, designed for this cleaning exercise.
